claim: Calling a lost dog's name can become a conditioned fear trigger causing it to bolt, because previous strangers have already used calling + direct approach to try to capture it.
stance_target: fear escalation dynamics — evasion conditioning via prior capture attempts
scope_conditions: Dogs in fight-or-flight mode; effect compounded by multiple prior failed capture attempts
quoted_support: It's likely that other people (who encountered the loose dog) have already tried to capture him and calling him has become a 'trigger' that causes him to automatically bolt in fear when anyone, including his owner, calls him.

claim: During peak adrenaline/fight-or-flight state, the olfactory section of a dog's brain shuts down, making food lures temporarily ineffective.
stance_target: fear escalation — olfactory shutdown during peak cortisol state
scope_conditions: Peak fight-or-flight state; olfactory function partially restored as dog calms
quoted_support: When dogs are in a full fight or flight mode and their adrenaline is flowing, the olfactory section of their brain closes down. That's why sometimes when you try to feed a hotdog to a panicked dog it won't eat it.

claim: Cortisol flooding during fight-or-flight temporarily blocks memory, so a panicked dog may not recognize its owner's face or voice until it has calmed sufficiently for scent detection to work.
stance_target: cortisol-mediated temporary memory loss in panicked dogs
scope_conditions: Fear-induced fight-or-flight; memory restoration occurs as cortisol drops and parasympathetic system re-engages
quoted_support: High cortisol being released in his brain... the dog will most likely run, even from his own family... Many dogs do not recognize their owner/guardian initially but do once the dog has calmed down enough and could detect her scent.

claim: Calming signals (sitting/lying down, avoiding direct eye contact, sideways posture, food-dropping with crinkly bags) activate the dog's parasympathetic nervous system, reducing cortisol and enabling approach.
stance_target: calming signals as the scientifically-based mechanism for approaching panicked dogs
scope_conditions: Dogs in fight-or-flight; technique developed by Turid Rugaas (Norwegian dog trainer)
quoted_support: 'Calming Signals' work so well—they help to activate the parasympathetic nervous system which calms a dog down to the point where he may respond to food and even to the owner's scent (which the dog can only detect once he is calm).

claim: Singing to a lost dog (rather than calling its name, whistling, clapping) is recommended because it produces a unique sound not previously used by failed capture attempts, avoiding trigger conditioning.
stance_target: behavioral protocol for approaching a conditioned-fear lost dog
scope_conditions: Dogs with multiple prior failed capture attempts; singing provides owner-voice exposure without triggering conditioned bolt response
quoted_support: SING TO YOUR DOG... singing to your dog will accomplish two things: (1) it will get your voice heard by your dog and (2) IT WILL SOUND DIFFERENT TO YOUR DOG THAN THE OTHER SOUNDS STRANGERS WHO'VE TRIED TO GRAB YOUR DOG HAVE USED (calling out, hand clapping, whistling).

claim: Direct eye contact combined with walking toward a panicked dog is a dominant/predator-like approach that triggers bolting — sideways, non-frontal body orientation is required.
stance_target: body language requirements for approaching panicked dogs — never-chase rule
scope_conditions: Dogs in fight-or-flight; applies equally to owners and strangers
quoted_support: People have tried to call the dog as they looked directly at the dog and walked towards it, an action that is dominant and frightening to a dog that is in the fight or flight mode.
